Title: Iterative effects: trails, decay, feedback
Summary: Declare a sampler with hint_previous_frame to read the last frame's rendered output. Unlocks trails, decay echoes, feedback blur — anything that builds on its own history.
Body:
## What `hint_previous_frame` does

A sampler2D declared with `hint_previous_frame` reads from a back-buffer that holds the **previous frame's rendered output**. Bleepforge runs a ping-pong cycle behind the scenes: two color-attached framebuffers swap roles each frame, and the sampler always points at whichever was "front" last time.

```glsl
uniform sampler2D PREV : hint_previous_frame, filter_linear;
uniform float decay : hint_range(0.0, 1.0) = 0.85;

void fragment() {
    vec4 cur = ...;
    vec4 prev = texture(PREV, SCREEN_UV);
    COLOR = mix(cur, prev, decay);
}
```

The canonical trails pattern: each frame mixes a small amount of fresh content with a large amount of the previous frame. The image "remembers" itself, fading over time at a rate set by `decay`.

## What it unlocks

- **Trails / smears** — moving content leaves color streaks that fade. The LSD/DMT signature of "head movement carrying afterimages." Pair with a small per-frame UV drift so the trail wanders rather than locking to the source position.
- **Decay glow** — bright pixels echo themselves at progressively dimmer levels until they fade out. Makes light sources feel alive.
- **Feedback blur** — combine a small UV jitter with a high decay value to produce a long-tail blur that's cheaper than a real kernel.
- **Iterative refinement** — any algorithm that converges over multiple frames (cellular automata, reaction-diffusion, etc.) can run "in-place" by reading and writing through this sampler.

## How the back buffer initializes

On the very first frame after a shader compiles or after a canvas resize, the back-FBO is freshly cleared to transparent black. Sampling `PREV` returns zero everywhere until trails build up over the next few frames. For most shaders this is invisible (the buildup is fast); for shaders that explicitly multiply by the previous value, the first ~30 frames may look different from the steady state.

## Filter and wrap on the back buffer

`filter_*` and `repeat_*` hints on the same sampler apply to the back-buffer texture:

- **Default is `filter_linear` + `repeat_disable`** (CLAMP_TO_EDGE) because trails almost always want smooth sub-pixel sampling. Add `filter_nearest` if you want chunky pixel-art trails instead.
- `repeat_enable` makes UV samples wrap — useful if your trail UV drifts past the edge and you want it to come back in the other side.

## The Godot portability trade-off

`hint_previous_frame` is **Bleepforge-specific**. Godot doesn't recognize it natively. Two options when you ship the shader:

1. **Comment-toggle**: write the hint as `uniform sampler2D PREV /* : hint_previous_frame */;` and uncomment it only in Bleepforge for testing. In Godot you wire ping-pong manually with a [SubViewport](https://docs.godotengine.org/en/stable/classes/class_subviewport.html) feeding a CanvasLayer that displays both layers.
2. **Two source files**: maintain a Bleepforge preview copy and a Godot deployment copy. Diverges over time, probably not worth it.

The SubViewport setup in Godot is a one-time scene change — once you have a CanvasLayer that's pointed at a SubViewport rendering of itself with one frame's lag, you can swap any shader into it without re-doing the wiring.

## The runtime cost

Three draw calls per frame instead of two:

1. Pass 1 (passthrough draws the bound texture to the front FBO)
2. Pass 2 (user shader writes over it, sampling the back FBO via `PREV`)
3. Final blit (front FBO → canvas)

At the preview canvas's typical size (a few hundred pixels square at DPR 1-2), the cost is negligible — the GPU spends more time rasterizing your shader than on the extra draws. Two extra FBO textures sit in VRAM (each `RGBA8` at canvas size; a few MB at most).

## Authoring tips

- **Add a small UV drift** when sampling `PREV` — `texture(PREV, SCREEN_UV + vec2(sin(TIME * 0.5), cos(TIME * 0.3)) * 0.002)` — so trails wander instead of locking to the source position. Static UV sampling produces "ghosts in place" rather than "trails" and tends to look like a graphics glitch.
- **Cap your decay below 1.0**. At decay = 1.0 the previous frame is sampled at full weight; combined with any non-trivial source content, the image will saturate to white (or whatever the brightest sample was) and never decay. Practical range: 0.7–0.95.
- **Reset the trip cleanly**. When the player's drug-software item wears off, you may want to clear the trail buffer rather than let it slowly fade. Bleepforge can't do that mid-run today (no API to clear the back FBO programmatically) — you'd reset by adjusting your master `intensity` or `decay` uniform.
- **Pair with the existing test image**: pick a screenshot with motion-readable content (an NPC, a character, an animated background) and crank decay to see the trail effect clearly. Static images make trails invisible because there's nothing for them to lag behind.
